notes.section_title: Why do we need Political Parties?

content.text: A political party is a group of people who come together to contest elections and hold power in the government. They agree on some policies and programmes for the society with a view to promote the collective good.

content.title: Three Components of a Political Party
items: The leaders: Those who handle the core decision-making and lead the party.
The active members: Committed individuals who work for the party's growth and mobilization.
The followers: Supporters who believe in the party's ideology and vote for its candidates.

content.title: Partisanship
content.text: Parties reflect fundamental political divisions in a society. A party is known by which part it stands for, which policies it supports, and whose interests it upholds. Partisanship is the tendency to take a side and inability to take a balanced view on an issue.
Political parties perform a series of critical functions in a democracy:

content.title: Key Functions
items: Contest Elections: In most democracies, elections are fought mainly among candidates put up by political parties.
Policies and Programmes: Parties put forward different policies from which voters choose.
Making Laws: Parties play a decisive role in the legislature, following the direction of party leadership.
Form and Run Governments: Parties recruit leaders, train them, and make them ministers.
Role of Opposition: Parties that lose role-play as critics of the government's failures and wrong policies.
Shape Public Opinion: They raise and highlight issues through their lakhs of members and pressure group extensions.
Access to Government Machinery: Parties provide citizens access to government machinery and welfare schemes.

content.text: Modern representative democracies cannot exist without political parties because large-scale societies need an agency to gather different views on various issues and to present these to the government.

content.title: Imagine a Situation Without Parties
content.text: Every candidate would be independent, making no major policy promises to the country. No one would be responsible for how the whole country is run. Political parties fulfill the need for a responsible government.

notes.section_title: How many parties should we have?

content.title: Types of Systems
items: One-Party System: Only one party is allowed to control and run the government (e.g., China). This is not considered a democratic option.
Two-Party System: Power usually changes between two main parties (e.g., USA, UK).
Multi-Party System: Several parties compete for power and have a reasonable chance of coming to power (e.g., India).

content.text: A multi-party system allows a variety of interests and opinions to enjoy political representation. However, it can sometimes lead to political instability.

content.title: Alliance or Front
content.text: When several parties in a multi-party system join hands for the purpose of contesting elections and winning power, it is called an alliance or a front. In India, examples include the NDA (National Democratic Alliance) and the UPA (United Progressive Alliance).
The Election Commission of India has laid down detailed criteria for a party to be recognized as a State or National party.

content.title: Recognition Criteria
items: State Party: Secures at least 6% of the total votes in a State Assembly election and wins at least two seats.
National Party: Secures at least 6% of the total votes in Lok Sabha elections OR in 4 states, AND wins at least four seats in the Lok Sabha.

content.title: National Party Profiles
items: Aam Aadmi Party (AAP): Formed in 2012; focuses on accountability, transparency, and clean administration; government in Delhi and Punjab.
Bahujan Samaj Party (BSP): Formed in 1984 under Kanshi Ram; seeks to represent Bahujan Samaj (Dalits, Adivasis, OBCs, minorities).
Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP): Founded in 1980; wants to build a strong India based on ancient culture and values (Hindutva).
Communist Party of India - Marxist (CPI-M): Founded in 1964; believes in Marxism-Leninism; supports social justice and opposes imperialism.
Indian National Congress (INC): Founded in 1885; centrist ideological orientation; emphasizes secularism and welfare of weaker sections.
National People's Party (NPP): Formed in 2013; first national party from North East India; focuses on regional development challenges.

content.title: Key Challenges
items: Lack of Internal Democracy: Concentration of power in one or few leaders at the top; absence of organizational meetings and internal elections.
Dynastic Succession: Top positions often controlled by members of one family, which is unfair to other members and bad for democracy.
Money and Muscle Power: Growing role of rich people and companies during elections; nomination of candidates with criminal backgrounds.
Lack of Meaningful Choice: Decline in ideological differences between parties; the same set of leaders often shift from one party to another.

content.title: Legal and Institutional Steps
items: Anti-Defection Law: Amendment to prevent elected representatives (MLAs/MPs) from changing parties after elections.
Affidavits: Sworn statements required by the Supreme Court from candidates regarding their property and criminal cases.
Organizational Elections: Election Commission order making internal elections and income tax returns mandatory.
State Funding: Suggestion for the government to give parties money or resources (petrol, paper) for election expenses.

content.title: The Citizen's Role
content.text: While legal solutions are important, two other ways exist: people putting pressure through agitations and petitions, and ordinary citizens joining political parties to improve them from within.

glossary.definition: A person who is strongly committed to a party, group, or faction, often leading to an inability to take a balanced view.
The political party that runs the government after winning the majority in elections.
A quality marked by a tendency to take a side and loyalty to one's own party's viewpoints.
Changing party allegiance from the party on which a person got elected to a different party.
A signed document submitted to an officer where a person makes a sworn statement regarding personal information like assets and criminal records.
A temporary alliance of several political parties forming a government when no single party has a clear majority.
A party given a unique election symbol and special facilities by the Election Commission after meeting specific vote and seat criteria.
When several parties join hands for the purpose of contesting elections and winning power together.

summary_points: Political parties are the most visible face of democracy, consisting of leaders, active members, and followers.
Parties perform vital functions such as contesting elections, formulating policies, making laws, and running the government machinery.
Representative democracies need political parties as agencies to aggregate diverse opinions and ensure a responsible government.
Countries may follow one-party, two-party, or multi-party systems, with India being a prime example of the latter.
The Election Commission classifies parties into 'National' or 'State' based on their performance in assembly or Lok Sabha elections.
Major national parties in India include the BJP, INC, BSP, AAP, CPI-M, and NPP, each with distinct ideologies.
Parties face challenges like lack of internal democracy, dynastic succession, corruption, and a lack of ideological choice for voters.
Reforms like the Anti-Defection Law and the mandatory filing of affidavits have been introduced to clean up political life.
Over-regulation can be counter-productive; lasting change comes through citizen participation and public pressure for transparency.
